summary: Gruber reviews the new aluminum Siri Remote that ships with the 2nd generation Apple TV 4K, declaring it a triumphant correction after six years of the despised black Siri Remote. He catalogs the old remote's many design failures — easy to pick up backwards, accidental trackpad clicks, fingerprint-magnet surface, invisible in the dark — and celebrates how the new design addresses every one. The new remote features a clickpad with touch surface, a jog-dial D-pad ring reminiscent of the iPod click wheel, better heft and size, and a cleverly repositioned Siri button. He notes a few minor complaints, particularly the lack of Find My support and swapped button positions causing muscle memory issues. The Apple TV 4K hardware update itself is modest, with the new remote being the real story.
key_points: The black Siri Remote was a clear design failure that persisted for six years, with problems including accidental clicks, symmetrical orientation, fingerprint-prone surfaces, and poor visibility in dark rooms
The new Siri Remote's clickpad with touch surface and iPod-like jog dial D-pad ring combine the best of touch interaction with physical button reliability
Moving the Siri button to the side is a smart design choice that separates meta-actions from on-screen controls and reduces clutter
The new Apple TV 4K hardware is a minor upgrade over the 1st gen 4K model, with the remote being the compelling reason to buy
The absence of Find My support via U1 chip is a puzzling omission for a device notorious for getting lost
money_quotes: I never liked that black Siri Remote, but over the years — six! — that I've been using it, I've grown to truly resent it. It is offensive, because it's so clearly a bad design.
The new aluminum Siri Remote that ships with the new 2nd generation Apple TV 4K looks, feels, and acts like the black Siri Remote never happened.
Making the remote feel like an iPod in your hand suddenly seems so obvious. That's a sign of a great idea — that it feels obvious once you've experienced it.
There was no menu that came up when you hit the Menu button — it took you back, always.
Apple chose a surface texture that looks bad as soon as it is touched, unless you wear gloves while watching TV, in which case you won't be able to use the capacitive trackpad.
"Hey Siri, where is the Apple TV remote?" seems like a natural thing to do.
If you're an Apple TV user, break out the champagne. This new Siri Remote is good.
key_insight: The new Siri Remote succeeds by treating the despised black remote as if it never happened, returning to the design lineage of the old aluminum remote while adding the best touch-interaction ideas in a form that actually works.
